author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew VIII. 14, 15.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And when Jesus was come into Peter’s house, He saw his wife’s mother laid and sick of a fever:and He touched her hand, and the fever left her, and she arose and ministered unto Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Mark adds also, “immediately,”meaning to declare the time as well; but this evangelist hath set down only the miracle, without signifying besides the time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whereas the others say, that she that lay ill did also entreat Him, this too he hath passed over in silence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this comes not of any dissonance, but the one of brevity, the other of exact narrative.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But for what intent did He go into Peter’s house?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As it seems to me, to take food.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This at least is declared when it is said,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “She arose and ministered unto Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He used to visit His disciples (as Matthew likewise, when He had called him), so honoring them and making them more zealous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But do thou mark, I pray thee, herein also Peter’s reverence towards Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though he had his wife’s mother at home lying ill, and very sick of a fever, he drew Him not into his house, but waited first for the teaching to be finished, then for all the others to be healed; and then when He had come in, besought Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus from the beginning was he instructed to prefer the things of all others to his own.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore neither doth he himself bring Him in, but He entered of His own accord (after the centurion had said, “I am not worthy that Thou shouldest come under my roof”): to show how much favor He bestowed on His disciple.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet consider of what sort were the houses of these fishermen; but for all that, He disdained not to enter into their mean huts, teaching thee by all means to trample under foot human pride.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And sometimes He heals by words only, sometimes He even stretches forth His hand, sometimes He doeth both these things, to bring into sight His way of healing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it was not His will always to work miracles in the more surpassing manner: it being needful for Him to be concealed awhile, and especially as concerned His disciples; since they out of their great delight would have proclaimed everything.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this was evident from the fact, that even after coming to the mount, it was needful to charge them that they should tell no man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having therefore touched her body, He not only quenched the fever, but also gave her back perfect health.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, the disease being an ordinary one, He displayed His power by the manner of healing; a thing which no physician’s art could have wrought.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For ye know that even after the departing of fevers, the patients yet need much time to return to their former health.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But then all took place at once.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not in this case only, but also in that of the sea.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither there did He quiet the winds only and the storm, but He also stayed at once the swelling of the waves; and this also was a strange thing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even if the tempest should cease, the waves continue to swell for a long time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But with Christ it was not so, but all at once was ended: and so it befell this woman also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also the evangelist, to declare this, said, “She arose and ministered unto Him;”which was a sign both of Christ’s power, and of the disposition of the woman, which she showed towards Christ.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And another thing together with these we may hence observe, that Christ grants the healing of some to the faith even of others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since in this case too, others besought Him, as also in the instance of the centurion’s servant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this grant He makes, when there is no unbelief in him that is to be healed, but either through disease he cannot come unto Him, or through ignorance imagines nothing great of Him, or because of His immature age.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “When the even was come, they brought unto Him many that were possessed with devils: and He cast out the spirits from them with a word, and healed all that were sick: that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the Prophet Esaias, that He took our infirmities, and bare our sicknesses.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the multitude, by this time growing in faith?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not even when the time pressed could they endure to depart, nor did they account it unseasonable to bring their sick to Him at eventide.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But mark, I pray thee, how great a multitude of persons healed the evangelists pass quickly over, not mentioning one by one, and giving us an account of them, but in one word traversing an unspeakable sea of miracles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then lest the greatness of the wonder should drive us again to unbelief, that even so great a people and their various diseases should be delivered and healed by Him in one moment of time, He brings in the prophet also to bear witness to what is going on: indicating the abundance of the proof we have, in every case, out of the Scriptures; such, that from the miracles themselves we have no more; and He saith, that Esaias also spake of these things; “He took our infirmities, and bare our sicknesses.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He said not, “He did them away,” but “He took and bare them;” which seems to me to be spoken rather of sins, by the prophet, in harmony with John, where he saith, “Behold the Lamb of God, that beareth the sin of the world.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then doth the evangelist here apply it to diseases?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Either as rehearsing the passage in the historical sense,or to show that most of our diseases arise from sins of the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if the sum of all, death itself, hath its root and foundation from sin, much more the majority of our diseases also: since our very capability of suffering did itself originate there.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Now when Jesus saw great multitudes about Him, He gave commandment to depart unto the other side.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou again His freedom from ostentation? in that as the others say, “He charged the devils not to say it was He,”so this writer saith, He repels the multitudes from Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now in so doing, He was at once both training us to be moderate,and at the same time allaying the envy of the Jews, and teaching us to do nothing for display.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He was not, we know, a healer to bodies only, but a curer also of the soul, and a teacher of self-restraint; by both disclosing Himself, both by putting away their diseases, and by doing nought for display.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because they indeed were cleaving unto Him, loving Him, and marvelling at Him, and desiring to look upon Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For who would depart from one who was doing such miracles?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Who would not long, were it only to see the face, and the mouth that was uttering such words?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not by any means in working wonders only was He wonderful, but even when merely showing Himself, He was full of great grace; and to declare this the prophet said, “Fairin beauty beyond the children of men.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if Esaias saith, “He hath no form nor comeliness,”he affirms it either in comparison of the glory of His Godhead, which surpasses all utterance and description; or as declaring what took place at His passion, and the dishonor which He underwent at the season of the cross, and the mean estate which throughout His life He exemplified in all respects.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Further: He did not first give “commandment to depart unto the other side,” nor until He had healed them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For surely they could not have borne it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As therefore on the mountain they not only continued with Him while exhorting them, but also when it was silence followed Him; so here too, not in His miracles only did they wait on Him, but also when He had ceased again, from His very countenance receiving no small benefit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if Moses had his face made glorious, and Stephen like that of an angel; consider thou our common Lord, what manner of person it was likely He would appear at such a time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Many now perchance have fallen into a passionate desire of seeing that form; but if we are willing we shall behold one far better than that.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if we can pass through our present life with Christian boldness,we shall receive Him in the clouds, meeting Him in an immortal and incorruptible body.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But observe how He doth not simply drive them away, lest He should hurt them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He did not say, “withdraw,” but “gave commandment to depart to the other side,” giving them to expect that He would surely come thither.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the multitudes for their part evinced this great love, and were following with much affection; but some one person, a slave of wealth, and possessed with much arrogance, approaches Him, and saith,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Master, I will follow Thee whithersoever Thou goest.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how great his arrogance?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as not deigning to be numbered with the multitude, and indicating that he is above the common sort, so he comes near.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because such is the Jewish character; full of unseasonable confidence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So too another afterwards, when all men were keeping silence, of his own accord springs up, and saith, “Which is the first commandment?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet nevertheless the Lord rebuked not his unseasonable confidence, teaching us to bear even with such as these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore He doth not openly convict them who are devising mischief, but replies to their secret thought, leaving it to themselves only to know that they are convicted, and doubly doing them good, first by showing that He knows what is in their conscience, next by granting unto them concealment after this manifestation, and allowing them to recover themselves again, if they will: which thing He doth in the case of this man also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he, seeing the many signs, and many drawn after Him, thought to make a gain out of such miracles; wherefore also he was forward to follow Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whence is this manifest?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: From the answer which Christ makes, meeting not the question, as it stands verbally, but the temper shown in its meaning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, “What?” saith He, “dost thou look to gather wealth by following me?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou not then that I have not even a lodging, not even so much as the birds have?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “the foxes,” saith He, “have holes, and the birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man hath not where to lay His head.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now these were not the words of one turning Himself away, but of one who while putting to the proof his evil disposition, yet permitted him (if he were willing with such a prospect) to follow Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to convince thee of his wickedness, when he had heard these things, and had been proved, he did not say, “I am ready to follow Thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in many other places also Christ is clearly doing this; He doth not openly convict, but by His answer He manifests the purpose of them that are coming unto Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus to him again that said, “Good Master,” and had thought by such flattery to gain His favor, according to his purpose He made answer, saying, “Why callest thou me good?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: There is none good but one, that is, God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when they said unto Him, “Behold, Thy mother and Thy brethren seek Thee;”forasmuch as these were under the influence of some human infirmity, not desiring to hear something profitable, but to make a display of their relationship to Him, and therein to be vainglorious; hear what He saith: “Who is my mother, and who are my brethren?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And again to His brethren themselves, saying unto Him, “Show thyself to the world,”and wishing thence to feed their vainglory, He said, “Your time” (so He speaks) “is always ready, but my time is not yet come.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in the opposite cases too He doth so; as in that of Nathanael, saying, “Behold an Israelite indeed, in whom is no guile.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And again, “Go and show John again those things which ye do hear and see.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither in this did He reply to the words, but to the intention of him that sent them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And with the people again in like manner, He addresses His discourse unto their conscience, saying, “What went ye out into the wilderness to see?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is because they were probably feeling about John, as though he had been a sort of easy and wavering person; to correct this their suspicion, He saith, “What went ye out into the wilderness to see?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: A reed shaken with the wind?” or, “a man clothed with soft raiment?” by both these figures declaring, that he was neither of himself a waverer, nor would be softened by any luxury.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus then in the present case also He makes His answer to their meaning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And see how in this also He shows forth great moderation: in that He said not, “I have it indeed, but despise it,” but “I have it not.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou what exact care goes along with His condescension?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Even as when He eats and drinks, when He seems to be acting in an opposite way to John, this too He doeth for the sake of the Jews’ salvation, or rather for that of the whole world, at once both stopping the mouths of the heretics,and desiring to win also more abundantly those of that day to Himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But a certain other one, we read, said unto Him,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Lord, suffer me first to go and bury my father.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou mark the difference? how one impudently saith, “I will follow Thee whithersoever Thou goest;” but this other, although asking a thing of sacred duty,saith, “Suffer me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet He suffered him not, but saith, “Let the dead bury their dead, but do thou follow me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in every case He had regard to the intention.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And wherefore did He not suffer him? one may ask.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because, on the one hand, there were those that would fulfill that duty, and the dead was not going to remain unburied; on the other, it was not fit for this man to be taken away from the weightier matters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But by saying, “their own dead,” He implies that this is not one of His dead.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that because he that was dead, was, at least as I suppose, of the unbelievers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now if thou admire the young man, that for a matter so necessary he besought Jesus, and did not go away of his own accord; much rather do thou admire him for staying also when forbidden.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Was it not then, one may say, extreme ingratitude, not to be present at the burial of his father?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If indeed he did so out of negligence, it was ingratitude, but if in order not to interrupt a more needful work, his departing would most surely have been of extreme inconsideration.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For Jesus forbad him, not as commanding to think lightly of the honor due to our parents, but signifying that nothing ought to be to us more urgent than the things of Heaven, and that we ought with all diligence to cleave to these, and not to put them off for ever so little, though our engagements be exceeding indispensable and pressing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what can be more needful than to bury a father? what more easy? since it would not even consume any long time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if one ought not to spend even as much time as is required for a father’s burial, nor is it safe to be parted even so long from our spiritual concerns; consider what we deserve, who all our time stand off from the things that pertain to Christ, and prefer things very ordinary to such as are needful, and are remiss, when there is nothing to press on us?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And herein too we should admire the instructivenessof His teaching, that He nailed him fast to His word, and with this freed him from those endless evils, such as lamentations, and mournings, and the things that follow thereafter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For after the burial he must of necessity proceed to inquire about the will, then about the distribution of the inheritance, and all the other things that follow thereupon; and thus waves after waves coming in succession upon him, would bear him away very far from the harbor of truth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause He draws him, and fastens him to Himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if thou still marvellest, and art perplexed, that he was not permitted to be present at his father’s burial; consider that many suffer not the sick, if it be a father that is dead, or a mother, or a child, or any other of their kinsmen, to know it, nor to follow him to the tomb; and we do not for this charge them with cruelty nor inhumanity: and very reasonably.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, on the contrary, it were cruelty to bring out to the funeral solemnity men in such a state.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if to mourn and be afflicted in mind for them that are of our kindred is evil, much more our being withdrawn from spiritual discourses.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this same cause He said elsewhere also, “No man having put his hand to the plough, and looking back, is fit for the kingdom of Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And surely it is far better to proclaim the kingdom, and draw back others from death, than to bury the dead body, that is nothing advantaged thereby; and especially, when there are some to fulfill all these duties.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nothing else then do we learn hereby, but that we must not wantonly lose any, no not the smallest time, though there be ten thousand things to press on us; but to set what is spiritual before all, even the most indispensable matters, and to know both what is life, and what is death.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since many even of them that seem to live are nothing better than dead men, living as they do in wickedness; or rather these are worse than the dead; “For he that is dead,” it is said, “is freed from sin,”but this man is a slave to sin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For tell me not of this, that he is not eaten of worms, nor lies in a coffin, nor hath closed his eyes, nor is bound in graveclothes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, for these things he undergoes more grievously than the dead, no worms devouring him, but the passions of his soul tearing him to pieces more fiercely than wild beasts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if his eyes be open, this too again is far worse than having closed them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For those of the dead see no evil thing, but this man is gathering unto himself diseases without number, while his eyes are open.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whereas the other lies in a coffin, unmoved by anything, this one is buried in the tomb of his innumerable distempers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But thou seest not his body in a state of decay.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what of that?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since before his body, his soul is corrupted and destroyed, and undergoes greater rottenness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the other stinketh a fewdays, but this for the whole of his life exhales evil odors, having a mouth more foul than sewers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And so the one differs from the other, by just so much as this, that the dead indeed undergoes that decay only which comes of nature, but this man together with that, brings in also that rottenness which is from intemperance, devising each day unnumbered causes of corruption.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But is he borne on horseback?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what of that?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, so is the other on a couch.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what is very hard, while the other is seen by no one in his dissolution and decay, but hath his coffin for a veil, this man is going about everywhere with his evil savor, bearing about a dead soul in his body as in a tomb.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if one could but once see a man’s soul who is living in luxury and vice, thou wouldest perceive that it is far better to lie bound in a grave than to be rivetted by the chains of our sins; and to have a stone laid over thee, than that heavy coverof insensibility.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore above all things it behooves the friends of these dead men, seeing that they are past feeling, to come near to Jesus in their behalf, as Mary then did in the case of Lazarus.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Though he “stinketh,” though he be “dead four days,” do not despair, but approach, and remove the stone first.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for then thou shalt see him lying as in a tomb, and bound in his grave clothes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if ye will, let it be some one of them that are great and distinguished, whom we bring before you.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, fear not, for I will state the example without a name: or rather, though I should mention the name, not even so need there be any fear: for who ever fears a dead man? seeing that whatever one may do, he continues dead, and the dead cannot injure the living either little or much.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then behold their head bound up.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed, when they are for ever drunken, even as the dead by their many wrappers and grave-clothes, so are all their organs of sense closed and bound up.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou wilt look at their hands too, thou shalt see these again bound to their belly, like those of the dead, and fastened about not with grave-clothes, but what is far more grievous, with the bands of covetousness: obtaining as they do no leave from her to be stretched out for alms-giving, or for any other of such like good deeds; rather she renders them more useless than those of the dead.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wouldest thou also see their feet bound together?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See them again fastened about with cares, and for this cause never able to run unto the house of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hast thou seen the dead? behold also the embalmer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Who then is the embalmer of these?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The devil, who carefully fastens them about, and suffers not the man any longer to appear a man, but a dry stock.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For where there is no eye, nor hands, nor feet, nor any other such thing, how can such an one appear a man?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Even so may we see their soul also swaddled up, and rather an imagethan a soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Forasmuch then as they are in a sort of senseless state, being turned to dead men, let us in their behalf draw nigh unto Jesus, let us entreat Him to raise them up, let us take away the stone, let us loosen the grave clothes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if thou take away the stone, that is, their insensibility to their own miseries, thou wilt quickly be able to bring them also out of the tomb; and having brought them out, thou wilt more easily rid them of their bonds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then shall Christ know thee, when thou art risen, when unbound; then will He call thee even unto His own supper.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As many therefore of you as are friends of Christ, as many as are disciples, as many as love him that is gone, draw near unto Jesus, and pray.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even though his ill savor abound and be ever so intense, nevertheless not even so should we, his friends, forsake him, but so much the rather draw near; even as the sisters of Lazarus then did; neither should we leave interceding, beseeching, entreating, until we have received him alive.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if we thus order our own affairs, and those of our neighbors, we shall also attain speedily unto the life to come; unto which may we all attain, by the grace and love to man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory forever and ever.
